title: Vanity Table with Shelf Hutch
summary: A 1x12-leg vanity table topped with a two-shelf hutch built for a light-up mirror — a makeup vanity, not a bathroom fixture, sized around your own mirror rather than a fixed one.
description:
The base here is genuinely simple — two 1x12 leg boards pocket-holed straight to the underside of the tabletop with a 1/2" overhang, plus a pair of 1x4 aprons measured directly off the assembled legs rather than cut to a fixed number up front. **Measure the actual distance between your two legs at the tabletop before cutting the aprons** — this is what keeps the base square even if the tabletop overhang varies slightly from board to board.

The hutch on top holds a light-up mirror rather than being decorative on its own, and it's built as two separate shelf sections joined by a top board before the whole hutch connects to the base with screws. **Pocket holes work for the hutch, but plain brad nails and glue are just as good and easier to hide** — the plan is explicit that the hutch carries no structural load, so there's no reason to over-build it the way the base needs to be built.

Build both shelf sections first, then add the top, and only connect the finished hutch to the base last.

materials.name: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.note: Measure the actual distance between the legs at the tabletop before cutting.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: Every piece keeps its board's full width, so only the lengths change. From the single 10 ft 1x12 cut the 53" tabletop first, then the two 29-1/4" legs from what is left — that is 111-1/2" of parts out of 120", so there is no room for a mis-cut. From the two 8 ft 1x8s cut the 53" hutch top, four hutch sides and dividers at 20-3/4", and two hutch shelves at 11-3/4"; those seven pieces fill both boards with barely an inch to spare, so lay them out before you start cutting. Leave the 1x4 aprons uncut for now — they are measured off the assembled base two steps from here, and the 50-1/2" in the cut list is only what they should come to. Square every cut and label the pieces.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Lay the 53" tabletop face down on a flat surface. Stand a 29-1/4" leg board on each end, set in 1/2" from the end so the top overhangs each leg by that much — with 3/4" leg stock that leaves exactly 50-1/2" of clear space between the inside faces of the legs, which is the apron length in the cut list, so a leg that drifts costs you at the apron. Drill three pocket holes across the top end of each leg, then fasten to the underside of the top with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue. Check each leg square to the top with a speed square before the second screw goes in, since a pocket screw will happily pull a leg out of square as it seats.

steps.title: Attach the aprons
steps.body: Measure the actual distance between the two legs at the tabletop and cut both aprons to that number — it should come to 50-1/2", and if it does not, your measurement wins over the cut list. Drill pocket holes at each end of each apron and along one long edge. Fasten the first apron up under the tabletop at the front, using the edge holes into the top and the end holes into each leg, with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue; then repeat at the back. The two aprons are the only thing stopping this base racking, so they go on before anything else touches it.

steps.title: Build the hutch shelf sections
steps.body: Each hutch section is two 20-3/4" verticals with an 11-3/4" shelf between them, which makes the finished section 13-1/4" wide overall. Build both the same way flat on a table, gluing and driving 1-1/4" brad nails through the verticals into the shelf ends, and set the shelf at whatever height suits what you mean to keep there. Check each section for square before the glue sets and compare the two against each other. The hutch carries no structural load, so nails and glue are genuinely enough here — pocket holes would only add holes to hide.

steps.title: Attach the hutch top
steps.body: Stand the two finished sections on edge and lay the 53" hutch top across them. Position them so the top overhangs a little at each end and leaves an open span between the sections for the light-up mirror — with two 13-1/4" sections on a 53" top, that span comes to about 26-1/2" if the sections sit flush to the ends, so measure your own mirror and adjust the positions from there before anything is fastened. Glue and nail down through the top into the end of every vertical with 1-1/4" brads, keeping the back edges of the top and the sections flush.

steps.title: Connect the hutch to the base
steps.body: Set the hutch on the tabletop with its back edge flush with the back of the 11-1/4"-deep top. The hutch is 7-1/4" deep, so that leaves 4" of clear tabletop in front of it to actually work on. Drive screws up through the tabletop from underneath into the bottom end of each hutch vertical — going up from below keeps every fastener off the show surface. Check the hutch stands square to the top from the side before the last screws go in.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand everything, easing the front edge of the tabletop and the front edges of the hutch shelves where arms will rest. The pocket holes on the aprons face inward and can simply be left as they are. Prime and paint, or stain and seal with poly, giving the tabletop an extra coat since it takes the most wear and the most spills.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Vanity table with a shelf hutch for a light-up mirror
